title.en: Training & Tuning Models on Vertex AI

description.en: Run custom training jobs on managed GPUs, search hyperparameters with Vizier, stop wasteful trials early, and tune Gemini with supervised fine-tuning when prompting isn't enough.

story_variants.en:
## Your Code, Google's GPUs

Training on a laptop ends the day the dataset outgrows RAM or the epoch outgrows your patience. **Vertex AI custom training** takes your training code — as a Python package or, more commonly, a container image — and runs it on hardware you specify per job: `n2-standard-16` for classic ML, `a2`/`a3` machines with A100/H100 GPUs for deep learning, or TPU slices for the largest work. The job is ephemeral: provision, train, write artifacts to GCS, tear down. You pay for training minutes, not for an idle GPU box somebody forgot.

The anatomy of a `CustomJob`:

- **Worker pool spec** — machine type, accelerators, replica count (multi-worker for distributed training).
- **Container** — a prebuilt Vertex training container (PyTorch, TensorFlow, XGBoost, scikit-learn) or your own image from Artifact Registry.
- **Environment contract** — Vertex injects `AIP_MODEL_DIR`, the GCS path where your code must write the final model; downstream services (registry, endpoints) pick it up from there.
- **Service account** — the job's identity for reading training data from GCS/BigQuery; least privilege applies to robots doing math, too.

> **Callout:** The container-in, artifact-out contract is the whole trick. Because a training job is just 'run this image with these env vars', the same image runs identically in CI smoke tests, one-off jobs, and pipeline steps (lesson 10).

## Hyperparameter Tuning: Search, Don't Guess

Learning rate, batch size, tree depth, dropout — the knobs that decide whether the same architecture lands at 71% or 84%. A Vertex **hyperparameter tuning job** wraps your CustomJob and runs many **trials**, each with parameter values chosen by **Vizier**, Google's black-box optimization service. Your code's obligations are small: accept parameters as CLI flags, and report the objective metric back (via the `cloudml-hypertune` library / metric reporting hook).

| Search strategy | How it picks the next trial | When |
|---|---|---|
| Grid | Every combination | Tiny, discrete spaces; exhaustive audits |
| Random | Uniform sampling | Cheap baseline; embarrassingly parallel |
| Bayesian (Vizier default) | Model past results, pick promising regions | The default — fewer trials to a better optimum |

Two budget controls do the real cost engineering:

- **Parallelism** — running 10 trials at once is fast but blind (they can't learn from each other); Bayesian search benefits from some sequentiality.
- **Early stopping** — kill trials whose learning curves look doomed, and stop the whole study when the best result hasn't improved for a set number of trials — the *patience* pattern. That exact loop, patience and all, is this lesson's challenge, because you should be able to implement your convergence criterion, not just check a box.

> **Pitfall:** Tuning on a metric you don't actually care about is the classic silent failure. Optimizing validation accuracy on an imbalanced fraud dataset yields a model that says 'not fraud' beautifully. Choose the objective metric with the same care as the architecture.

## Tuning Foundation Models: A Different Sport

For Gemini-class models you don't retrain from scratch — you adapt:

- **Prompting first.** System instructions, few-shot examples, structured output. Zero infrastructure, instant iteration. Exhaust this before anything below.
- **Supervised fine-tuning (SFT)** on Vertex: a JSONL dataset of input/output pairs teaches the model your format, tone, or domain vocabulary. Uses parameter-efficient techniques (LoRA-family) under the hood; you get a tuned model version deployable like any other, priced as a tuning job plus serving.
- **Distillation**: use a large model's outputs to train a smaller, cheaper one for a narrow task.
- **RAG / grounding** (lesson 12) when the problem is *knowledge freshness*, not style — tuning bakes knowledge in at a point in time; retrieval keeps it current.

Decision rule that saves the most money: **tuning fixes 'how it answers'; retrieval fixes 'what it knows'; more/better prompting fixes most things people attempt tuning for.**

## Experiment Hygiene

Vertex AI **Experiments** tracks runs, parameters, and metrics; **TensorBoard** instances visualize learning curves; the **ML Metadata** store records lineage (which dataset + which code produced which artifact). None of it is glamorous, and all of it is the difference between 'we can reproduce the model in prod' and archaeology. Log every trial's parameters and objective — your future self is the audience.

## What You'll Build

A deterministic tuning-study simulator: iterate trials in order, track the best objective, apply patience-2 early stopping, and report the winner. It's Vizier's outer loop with the stochastic parts removed — the part *you* are responsible for understanding when the bill for 200 GPU-hours of trials lands.

description.en: Ephemeral compute: pay for training minutes, keep only the artifact.
Vizier proposes, trials report back, the posterior sharpens — Bayesian search in a loop.

animation_sequence.code:
from google.cloud import aiplatform as aip
job = aip.CustomJob.from_local_script(
    display_name="churn-train",
    script_path="train.py",
    container_uri="us-docker.pkg.dev/vertex-ai/training/pytorch-gpu.2-4:latest",
    machine_type="n1-standard-8",
    accelerator_type="NVIDIA_TESLA_T4")
job.run()

explanation.en: from_local_script packages your file into a prebuilt training container.
Prebuilt containers cover PyTorch/TF/XGBoost/sklearn; bring your own image for anything else.
Hardware is a per-job argument — a T4 today, an A100 for the big run, no cluster to keep.

left.label: Fine-tune Gemini so it knows this week's product catalog

right.label: Ground/RAG for fresh knowledge; tune for format, tone, and domain style

description.en: Tuning bakes knowledge in at a point in time — retrieval keeps it current.

lines: Early stopping: no improvement in 2 trials. Study complete.

description.en: The convergence log your challenge reproduces exactly.

instruction.en: Simulate a hyperparameter tuning study with early stopping. TRIALS is an ordered list of (trial_number, accuracy). Iterate in order, tracking the best accuracy. A trial with accuracy strictly greater than the best so far prints 'trial <n>: acc=<a> (new best)' (accuracy with 2 decimals) and resets the no-improvement counter; otherwise print 'trial <n>: acc=<a>' and increment the counter. When the counter reaches PATIENCE (2), print 'early stopping triggered after trial <n>' and stop — later trials never run. Finally print 'best: trial <n> with acc=<a>'. Implement run_study(trials, patience) returning (best_trial, best_acc, last_trial_run) and do the printing inside it. Print nothing else.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
TRIALS = [
    (1, 0.71),
    (2, 0.78),
    (3, 0.83),
    (4, 0.82),
    (5, 0.80),
    (6, 0.84),
]
PATIENCE = 2

def run_study(trials, patience):
    # TODO: track best_acc, best_trial, no_improve counter
    # - improvement (acc > best_acc): print 'trial <n>: acc=<a> (new best)', reset counter
    # - otherwise: print 'trial <n>: acc=<a>', counter += 1
    # - if counter == patience: print 'early stopping triggered after trial <n>' and break
    # After the loop print 'best: trial <n> with acc=<a>'
    # Format every accuracy with f'{acc:.2f}'. Return (best_trial, best_acc, last_trial_run).
    pass

run_study(TRIALS, PATIENCE)
practice_challenges.expected_output:
trial 1: acc=0.71 (new best)
trial 2: acc=0.78 (new best)
trial 3: acc=0.83 (new best)
trial 4: acc=0.82
trial 5: acc=0.80
early stopping triggered after trial 5
best: trial 3 with acc=0.83

en: Initialize best_acc to a value below any accuracy (e.g. -1.0) and no_improve to 0.
Trial 6 (0.84) must never print — the break after trial 5 happens first. That's the point of patience.
Use f'trial {n}: acc={acc:.2f}' so 0.80 keeps its trailing zero.

practice_challenges.test_code:
import io, contextlib
_buf = io.StringIO()
with contextlib.redirect_stdout(_buf):
    _res = run_study(TRIALS, PATIENCE)
assert _res == (3, 0.83, 5)
with contextlib.redirect_stdout(io.StringIO()):
    _r2 = run_study([(1, 0.5), (2, 0.6), (3, 0.7)], 2)
assert _r2 == (3, 0.7, 3)
with contextlib.redirect_stdout(io.StringIO()):
    _r3 = run_study([(1, 0.9), (2, 0.1), (3, 0.1), (4, 0.95)], 2)
assert _r3 == (1, 0.9, 3)

q.en: What does Vertex AI inject as AIP_MODEL_DIR into a custom training job?

en: The path to the training dataset
The GCS directory where your code must write the final model artifact

q.en: Which search strategy is Vizier's differentiator for hyperparameter tuning?

en: Bayesian optimization that models past trial results to pick promising regions
Training all trials on one GPU sequentially

q.en: Your Gemini app answers in the wrong tone/format but knows the facts. Cheapest effective fix to try first?

en: Full pretraining of a new model
Better prompting (system instructions + few-shot), then supervised fine-tuning if evals still fail

q.en: Why cap parallel trials in a Bayesian tuning study?

en: Fully parallel trials can't learn from each other's results, weakening the search
Vizier bills per parallel trial squared
